teaching.title: Function Overloading in C++
teaching.analogy: C++ function overloading is like a Swiss Army knife — same name, different tools for the job. The compiler picks the right blade at compile time based on what you hand it. C has no overloading; C++ adds it as a core feature.
teaching.content: C++ allows multiple functions with the same name if their parameter lists differ (count or types). The compiler resolves which to call at compile time. Return type alone cannot differentiate. Overloading works with any combination of types.

examples.title: Function Overloading

teaching.funFact: C++ overloading is implemented via 'name mangling' — the compiler encodes parameter types into the function's internal name. print(int) becomes _Z5printi in GCC. This is why C++ functions aren't directly callable from C! 🔧

teaching.title: Default Parameters
teaching.analogy: Default parameters are like restaurant menu items with a 'default' option — you can customize everything, or take the defaults and it still works. 'Order pizza()' gives you the default size. 'Order pizza(large)' overrides it.
teaching.content: C++ allows default parameter values: void greet(string name, string title='Dr.'). If not provided, default is used. Defaults must be rightmost parameters. Cannot combine with overloading of same function if ambiguous.

examples.title: Default Parameters
examples.explanation: Optional params with defaults — must be rightmost.

teaching.funFact: Python has default parameters too — but unlike C++, Python evaluates mutable defaults (like lists) only ONCE at function definition! This causes the famous 'mutable default argument' bug. C++ re-evaluates on each call! 🐛

mcq.q: C++ overloading — what must differ between overloaded functions?
mcq.explanation: Parameter List: Overloaded functions must have different parameter signatures. Return type alone doesn't differentiate — the compiler can't tell which to call from the call site alone.

mcq.q: void fn(int a, string b='x'). fn(5) is valid?
mcq.explanation: Default Used: fn(5) is valid. a=5, b defaults to 'x'. You can override: fn(5,'y') passes y for b.
Rightmost: fn(int a=1, int b) is invalid — b has no default but follows a default. Default params must trail: fn(int a, int b=2) is valid.

options: No — C has a single flat name space, no overloading
mcq.explanation: C No Overloading: C requires unique function names. printf, fprintf, sprintf are ALL different names. C++ added overloading — it's one of the key enhancements over C.

mcq.q: void add(int,int) and double add(int,int) — valid C++ overloading?
options: No — return type alone doesn't differentiate
mcq.explanation: Invalid: add(5,3) — compiler can't determine which overload from the call site. Must differ in parameter list.

coding.title: Overload count
coding.objective: 3 overloads: print(int), print(double), print(string). Call print(3.14) calls which?
coding.starterCode:
// print(int), print(double), print(string)
// print(3.14) calls: print(___)
coding.hint: 3.14 is a double literal → matches print(double).

coding.title: Default param
coding.objective: void greet(string name, string msg='Hello'). greet('Neo') uses msg=?
coding.starterCode:
// greet called with only name.
// msg defaults to: ___

coding.title: Default position
coding.objective: fn(int a=1, int b) — valid or invalid?
coding.starterCode: // void fn(int a=1, int b) — valid or invalid?
coding.hint: Default params must be rightmost. b has no default but follows a defaulted param.

debugging.brokenCode: void fn(int a=0, int b, int c=0)
options: b has no default but is in the middle — default params must be trailing. Fix: fn(int b, int a=0, int c=0) or fn(int a, int b, int c=0)
debugging.answer: b has no default but is in the middle — default params must be trailing. Fix: fn(int b, int a=0, int c=0) or fn(int a, int b, int c=0)
debugging.hint: All non-default params must come BEFORE default params. Can't have default, non-default, default.

teaching.title: C++ References — True Pass by Reference
teaching.analogy: A C++ reference is an alias — a second name for the same variable. Unlike C's pointer (which is an address you must dereference), a reference IS the variable. You write normal code but it operates on the original.
teaching.content: C++ reference: type& name. No dereferencing needed — reference IS the variable. int& x = n means x and n share memory. function(int& x) receives a reference — changes affect the caller. No nullptr, no pointer arithmetic.

examples.title: C++ Reference vs C Pointer
const Reference — Efficient Read-Only
examples.explanation: const ref: no copy, no modification.

teaching.funFact: C++ references are one of Bjarne Stroustrup's favorite additions over C — they allow clean by-reference passing without ugly pointer syntax. References can't be null, can't be reassigned, and can't do arithmetic. Safer than pointers! ✅

mcq.q: void fn(int& x){x=99;} int n=5; fn(n); n=?
mcq.explanation: 99: int& x is a reference to n. x=99 directly sets n to 99. No dereferencing needed — reference IS the variable.

mcq.q: C++ reference vs C pointer — key difference?
options: Reference: no * for access, can't be null, can't be reassigned; Pointer: explicit *, can be null
Pointer is C++, reference is C
mcq.explanation: References are cleaner: No dereferencing syntax, cannot be null, cannot be changed to refer to something else. Pointers are more powerful but riskier.

mcq.q: const string& name in function param — can you modify name inside?
mcq.explanation: Read-Only: const reference prevents modification inside the function. You get the efficiency of no-copy with the safety of immutability. Best for reading large objects.

mcq.q: To swap two ints using C++ references, caller writes:
mcq.explanation: Clean Call: void swap(int& a, int& b). Call: swap(x,y) — no & at call site! The & in the declaration handles referencing. Cleaner than C's swap(&x,&y).

coding.title: Reference modify
coding.objective: void set(int& x){x=42;} int n=0; set(n); n=?
coding.hint: int& x is a reference — x=42 sets n directly.

coding.title: Const ref param
coding.objective: void show(const string& s). Can you do s+='X' inside?
coding.hint: const reference = read-only. Modification is a compile error.

coding.title: Swap call
coding.objective: void swap(int& a, int& b). How do you call to swap x and y?
coding.hint: With references, call as swap(x,y) not swap(&x,&y). The & is in the declaration.

options: Passed by value — x is a copy. Change to: void addFive(int& x) to modify n
debugging.answer: Passed by value — x is a copy. Change to: void addFive(int& x) to modify n
debugging.hint: int x = value copy. int& x = reference. Use & to modify the caller's variable.

teaching.title: Lambda Expressions (C++11)
teaching.analogy: A lambda is a throwaway function — you write it inline right where you need it, use it once (or pass it around), and don't need to give it a name. Like writing a sticky note instead of a full document.
teaching.content: Lambda syntax: [capture](params) { body }. Capture [] copies or references outer variables. Stored in auto. Passed to STL algorithms (sort, find_if, for_each). The most modern C++ programming tool.

examples.title: Lambda Basics
Lambda Capture

teaching.funFact: C++ lambdas are actually syntactic sugar — the compiler converts them to anonymous class objects with operator() defined. Each lambda creates a unique, unnamed class type. This is why auto is needed to store them! 🔮

teaching.title: inline Functions
teaching.analogy: inline is a hint to the compiler: 'paste this function's code directly at the call site instead of making a function call.' Like copy-pasting instead of delegating — faster for tiny functions, no benefit for large ones.
teaching.content: inline keyword suggests the compiler to expand the function body at each call site, eliminating function call overhead. Used for very small, frequently called functions. Commonly used in header files. Modern compilers often inline automatically regardless.

examples.title: inline Functions
examples.explanation: Hint to avoid call overhead for tiny functions.

teaching.funFact: Modern optimizing compilers (GCC -O2, Clang -O2) ignore the inline keyword for optimization decisions — they inline what they determine is beneficial automatically. inline is now mostly useful for header-defined functions! 🤖

mcq.q: auto fn = [](int x){ return x*2; }. fn(5) = ?
mcq.explanation: Lambda: fn is a lambda that returns x*2. fn(5) = 5*2 = 10.
Capture Modes: [x] captures x by value (copy). [&x] captures by reference — changes to x inside lambda affect the outer x. [=] all by value, [&] all by reference.

options: Hint to compiler to expand function at call site (no call overhead)
mcq.explanation: inline Hint: Suggests the compiler to paste the function body at the call site. Eliminates function call overhead for tiny functions. Modern compilers may ignore it.

mcq.q: Which STL method commonly uses a lambda in C++?
mcq.explanation: sort with Lambda: sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](int a,int b){return a<b;}). The lambda is the comparator — replacing a standalone compare function with an inline definition.

coding.title: Lambda syntax
coding.objective: Lambda that doubles x: auto fn = ___(int x){ return x*2; };
coding.starterCode:
auto fn = ___(int x){ return x*2; };
cout << fn(7);
coding.hint: Lambda starts with [] for the capture list, then (params), then { body }.

coding.title: Lambda output
Capture by value
coding.objective: int x=10; auto fn=[x](){return x+5;}. fn() = ?
coding.hint: [x] captures x by value (copy of 10). x+5 = 15.

options: total not captured — lambda can't see outer variables unless captured. Use [&total] or [&].
debugging.answer: total not captured — lambda can't see outer variables unless captured. Use [&total] or [&].
debugging.hint: Lambda [] captures nothing. total is an outer variable. Use [&total] to capture by reference.

prediction.explanation: offset=10 captured by value. adjust(5)=5+10=15. adjust(20)=20+10=30.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE C++ COMPILER
teaching.analogy: The C++ Compiler instantiates every template, resolves every overload, inlines every candidate, and weaves every lambda into an anonymous class. Defeat it by proving you understand C++'s complete function model.
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 5 C++: overloading (params not return), default params rightmost, reference & vs value vs pointer, const&, swap via reference, lambdas [], capture modes, inline hint.

examples.title: Full C++ Function Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ Overloading: same name, diff param lists
✅ Default params: must be rightmost
✅ int& x — pass by reference (clean)
✅ const string& — efficient read-only
✅ swap(x,y) not swap(&x,&y) with refs
✅ auto fn = [](params){ body };
✅ [x] = by value, [&x] = by reference
✅ inline — suggestion, not command

teaching.funFact: C++20 introduced 'abbreviated function templates' using auto params: void print(auto x) — auto makes the function a template automatically. Function overloading is becoming less needed with this feature! 🚀

mcq.q: void fn(int& x){x*=2;} int n=7; fn(n); n=?
mcq.explanation: 14: int& x is a reference to n. x*=2 changes n directly. n = 7×2 = 14.

mcq.q: overload: fn(int,int) and fn(int,int,int). fn(1,2) calls?
mcq.explanation: Exact Match: fn(1,2) exactly matches fn(int,int). No ambiguity.

mcq.q: int x=5; auto fn=[&x](){x+=10;}; fn(); x=?
mcq.explanation: Capture by ref: [&x] captures x by reference. fn() runs x+=10. x = 5+10 = 15.

mcq.q: fn(int a, int b=5, int c) is valid C++?
options: No — c has no default but follows default b. All defaults must be trailing.
mcq.explanation: Invalid: Default parameters must be consecutive from the RIGHT. b has a default but c (after b) doesn't — compile error.

coding.title: Boss: Ref swap
coding.objective: void swp(int& a,int& b){int t=a;a=b;b=t;} x=3,y=8. After swp(x,y): x=?
coding.hint: swap exchanges via references. x gets y's value = 8.

coding.title: Boss: Lambda capture
coding.objective: int mul=3; auto fn=[mul](int x){return x*mul;}; fn(7)=?

options: Passed by value — count is never modified. Use: void increment(int& x) to actually increment count.
debugging.answer: Passed by value — count is never modified. Use: void increment(int& x) to actually increment count.
debugging.hint: int x = copy. int& x = reference. Need & to modify the caller's count.

prediction.explanation: [&n] captures n by reference. triple() runs n*=3. n=1→3→9.
